comment_content:
Extends from the content of COMP2011, this time focusing on OOP in C++.
Topics includes:
1) Const-ness (A bit annoying, but ok overall)
2) Constructors & Destructors (Needs time to understand)
3) Inheritance and Polymorphism (Very useful & Important)
4) Generic Programming (Useful, but syntax hell)
5) Binary Search Tree (A lot of recursion)
6) Hashing (Very straightforward)
7) rvalue Reference and Move Semantics (RIP if you don't get constructors and destructors)
8) Lambda and Function Pointer (Can be a bit weird, but worth ~10 points in this year's final)

comment_grading:
Weighting:
Lab:9% (6 Graded, 2 Attendance only)
PA: 24% (3 Total, 8% each)
Midterm: 27%
Final: 40%
*Graded labs are worth 3 points (1 based on ZINC result, 1 attendance, 1 from Canvas Quiz (Will only be given if attended Lab))
*Ungraded labs are worth 1 point (1 attendance)

Stats (My Raw Grade / Max Score / Weighted Score):
Labs: 19.75 / 20 / 8.875%
PA: 298 / 300 / 23.84%
Midterm: 93.25 / 100 / 25.1775%
Final: 103.5 / 110 (100 in practice, read note) / 41.4%
Total: 99.305%
Final Grade: A+
I think you can get A+ if total ≥ 90%, similar to COMP2011

Note: For some reason, the maximum score for the final is treated as 100. That extra 10 points is treated as bonus, i.e., you can get 44% at most (assuming you get full mark)

Personal Advice:
1) Canvas Quizzes in labs will reference lab content, but also the related topic, i.e. Remember to check both lab programme and the lecture notes, in case they ask for some specific behaviour.

2) Occasionally, the labs and PAs instructions may be a bit unclear, ask on Piazza! The TAs will usually reply within a day or two. Remember to check your code to make sure it fits updated requirements. (Forgot to check in PA1 and lost 2 pts :( )

3) Write you own test cases! The public ones on ZINC are not  always exhaustive.

4) Study well on Constructors, Destructors, Move Semantics & Copy Elision. This sem, ~10-15% in midterm and final are related to them. They will ask for the outputs which depends on which constructor is called. It is very easy to make a mistake, which in turn cascades into other mistakes. The question in the final has a point only awarded ONLY IF you get ALL output right.

5) The exams' programming questions doesn't tend to stray too far from lecture notes, but read the instructions and sample outputs carefully. Seriously, sample code + sample output + instructions are often 3 - 5 pages long, but implementation of tasks are fairly easy if you understand the question.

课程内容有些错位，这是因为诸如STL、static关键字以及namespace等实用性强的内容被安排在课外自学环节，而非作为授课部分详细讲解的核心内容。相比之下，面向对象编程（OOP）部分（p.s.仅限于对象生命周期）的讲解则非常详尽（占比70%以上，甚至有点过于详细，而完成UROP，甚至完成COMP 2012自己的Lab，PA，还是都不需要知道这么细致的关于面向对象编程的内容，感觉这门课的很多考题设计出来就是为了卷而卷，死抠犄角旮旯的C++语法细节，而不详细讲解C++在项目中更加实用的部分（string类库，STL容器，智能指针，多线程等等）。而且上课的时候没有提到类似虚函数表以及实现的的底层细节）。此外，数据结构占比差不多30%，讲了动态数组，单链表、双链表，栈、队列，二叉树（循环版本的前序中序后序遍历、递归版本的各类遍历、层序不包括），二叉搜索树 BST，哈希表（拉链法、开放地址）的增删改查，算法题讲得很少，完全不包含图，Trie 字典树，并查集，线段树 / 树状数组，LRU 缓存等更多的内容，感觉作为“数据结构”有点名不副实。
comment_teaching:
Core Issue Overview  
A notable disconnect exists between teaching and examination: Although Cecia provides detailed explanations of code specifics during lectures, there is a significant gap between the course content and the actual requirements of the examinations.  

Recommendations  
1. Necessity of self-directed learning: Relying solely on classroom content is inadequate for examination preparation. Additional independent study and practice are required to bridge the gap.  
2. Limitations of past examination papers: The provided past papers have limited alignment with the challenges of actual examinations. Complete reliance on them for preparation is not advisable.
comment_workload:
The workload for COMP 2012 is relatively heavy.
The first PA (Programming Assignment) is a review of pointers and linked lists, covering the final chapters of COMP 2011.
The second PA focuses on practicing object-oriented programming (OOP).
The third PA involves developing a small-scale ticket booking system, utilizing data structures and concepts such as binary trees, generic programming, and hash tables. This assignment is particularly demanding but serves as excellent practice for strengthening programming skills.

It is important to note that after submitting each PA, there are numerous hidden test cases to consider. I lost a few points due to some tricky hidden cases, but all my PA scores were above 90/100.

Additionally, each lab session is followed by a corresponding quiz, so thorough familiarity with the lecture slides (PPT) is essential.
comment_grading:
The overall grading composition for COMP 2012 is as follows:
Lab: 9%
Programming Assignment: 3 × 8% = 24%
Midterm Exam: 27% (Mean: 53/100)
Final Exam: 40%

My performance:
Lab: 8.775/9
Programming Assignment:
PA1: 96.8/100
PA2: 92.3/100
PA3: 99.1/100
Midterm Exam: 80.9/100 (Mean: 51.69/100, Upper Quartile: 71.95/100)
Final Exam: 57.35/110 (Mean: 47.5/110, Upper Quartile: 71.03/110) – I FUCKED UP THE FINAL. The additional 10 marks are added directly without adjustment, making it 57.35/100.

Overall: 76.614/100, Letter Grade: B+ (COMP 2011: A-)

The A range accounts for less than 15% of students, essentially reflecting an absolute grading system (overall score > 80). The midterm exam tested the object construction and destruction process with and without RVO (20 marks) and additional destruction details (10 marks). The final exam similarly covered object construction and destruction processes related to Move Semantics (16 marks). Most students were unable to finish the final exam. Additionally, the final included extensive content on Lambda expressions and function pointers (16 marks), which were only briefly covered in tutorials without in-depth study. The past papers provided on the official website are not fully representative, as the actual exam was significantly more difficult and broader in scope. I am in the CPEG program. If you are not in COMP or CPEG, it is advisable to consider taking an equivalent course at another university for credit transfer, if possible.

考试中要求学生像编译器一样手动书写长达30至40行的对象销毁流程代码，这在期中（有RVO和没有RVO的对象销毁）与期末考试（Moving Semantics输出对象生命周期的代码）中均有体现（占比20分以上，完全把考生当成编译器来搞，感觉挺无理取闹）并且期末考试大量考察上课没有大量练习（Lambda表达式），以及在Tutorial上一笔带过（Function Pointer）的内容（两者一共16分），感觉教考分离，内容丰富程度和考试方式远远不如COMP 2011，考试的时候问你“茴香豆的茴有几种写法”，现代版科举考试，（COMP 2011虽然比较难，但是可以学到很多基础算法方面的内容以及指针的底层原理，是真的可以入门C++以及编程的好课，COMP 2012纯粹无理取闹，为了卷而卷（A range < 15%），没苦硬吃！）如果不是Computer Science或者是Computer Engineering的话，请谨慎选择！如果是Computer Science或者是Computer Engineering的学生，也请仔细斟酌一下！

comment_workload:
For a 4-credit course, the workload is quite fair. I don't remember the exact number of labs, but we had three programming assignments (PAs), each of which required more than a day of focused work. Some of my friends, who were preparing for other important commitments, struggled to manage their time and ended up resorting to using other people's work. As a result, they either received close to 0% for those PAs due to inability to debug them or faced a -8% penalty for plagiarism.

The last PA is usually significantly harder than the first two. My advice is to work on your C++ skills daily for at least an hour and to manage your deadlines proactively.

Where can you learn? Practice with LeetCode questions to reinforce your basic knowledge, or dive into deep study sessions on cppreference (which is a fantastic resource). While books are helpful, they may not provide the same excitement, so I personally prefer YouTube videos. One great channel to check out is Cherno.
comment_grading:
It's better to take the course off-season. What I mean is that many cracked CS students enroll in this course during their first year in the spring, right after completing COMP2011. This often makes the grading curve tougher for someone like me, as I've transferred from business school to the Math CS track.

I believe that taking COMP2012 in the fall would offer a slightly easier grading experience. Also, it's essential to pay attention to the list of professors teaching the course. I personally would choose to take it with Professor Dimitris, even if that means being in a class with a lot of cracked CS students. For me, acquiring valuable skills from a good professor is more important than merely achieving a good grade.

2）單開一題的lambda Expression（3個小分題，總共9分）
[lambda Expression題目中有大量限制，如果真的想不出避開限制的做法，那就硬寫吧，好歹有幾分]
3）binary search Tree在大量的準備下難度也就那樣（佔20分）。
4) Template: 地獄，工作量等同於你的lab。需要你根據兩份cpp文件去改寫出兩份有template的header 文件和相對應的cpp文件。（佔22分）
5)hashing：以往是送分題，今年難度提升了，考的不是linear 和double hashing，是沒有見過的quadratic probing。 如果你熟悉hashing,那問題不大。（佔10分）

comment_teaching:
Dear students,

We believe that we were among the first batch of instructors (back some 10 years ago) who asked the university to help record our lectures and released them in a timely manner so that students had another way to review their understanding of the course materials.

In the beginning, it seems our lecture videos did us good. They are never meant to be a  replacement of attending lectures in person, which to us will serve various educational objectives such as real-time discussion (or Q&A), meeting/knowing your classmates, etc. We notice that some students want to have the videos sooner to review the lectures.  We are willing to balance the educational benefits of lecture videos and decide to release the lecture videos every 2 weeks (biweekly) now. The videos of lectures given in the first 2 weeks of this semester are now available at the Canvas' homepage.

Again the lecture videos should be used to review the lectures and not to replace the lectures.

====
Cecia and Brian are both good.
Very clear pronunciation, nicely edited powerpoints (except for lambdas and function pointers since the slides are taken from tutorial slides)
Although lecture video are released biweekly, students seems not to care about it and skip lectures, especially the post-midterm period.

Honorable mention: Desmond's video of "Recorded reviews on pointers and references"

<I can tell you all focus on the GRADING part.>

Basically the same as noted by others (and past semesters).
Minor changes on progress of teaching and content distribution between Mid-Term and Final.
You can find the full catalog in others posts. Here's a simplified version:
A) C++11 new features → range-for-loop / auto & lambdas / ...
B) function pointers (i.e. void (func*) (int, int)) [remark: said not to be tested this year, but know for your own good]
C) OOP: class & struct(ure) → access control / inheritance / *dynamic binding
D) templates
E) data structures → binary & binary search tree (AVL balancing algorithm is optional) / hashing [remark: basics like link-list are covered in COMP 2011]

* The recent exams favor Binary Trees though.

For your own interest, visit (https://github.com/ADMINGUOYU/COMP2012-HKUST-) to find out more about COMP 2012 this semester. (including my implementation of Labs and PAs, some random notes within class codes and sample programs in some of the mid-terms and finals.)

comment_content:
You know how in 1021 and 2011, you're basically doing "toy problems", where most labs and assigments ask you to finish making a game on the terminal, but nothing really practical?

Well, 2012 is finally the so called "practical programming course", where instead of focusing on foundational techniques, you are now tasked to implement programs using OOP concepts. While 1021 and 2011 are fun to learn, I never felt that I made anything "real" with most of their labs, and I think this course fixes this issue for sure. Do we still print stuff on the terminal? Yes that's true, which isn't super practical; but are the concepts we learned real world techniques? YES.

Topics in the finals:
Const-ness and Lambda
Constructors & Destructor
Inheritance and Polymorphism
Generic Programming
Binary Search Tree
Hashing
rvalue Reference and Move Semantics

I do wonder why they cut AVL tree and STL though, which are also quite useful to learn.
comment_teaching:
Yeah this guy is underrated, highly recommended to take his lectures if you can't take other good guys.

Clear voice, great understanding of the lectures, good teaching. Can get slightly boring sometimes, but you come to lectures to learn not to be entertained right?

Also probably one of friendliness professors I have ever met. It's so great to chat with him, and he constantly listens and give comments on things that helped a lot. He's a good guy :thumbsup:
comment_workload:
Something I like about the midterm and final are they don't test trivial and random shit, instead focus on making an entire system the way you like. If your codes can make the expected output, then it's good to go. 2011's final (in my experience) consists of random shit that is absolutely useless to ask, and forces you to do certain things the way they wanted, which sucks when I already figured out what to do but I have to fill in the blanks. I have attached this sem's 2012 final for your reference :)

Anyway, here's why the PAs suck:

1. Lack of public test cases

I get the idea that we should make our own test cases, but we should at least have some format to base off of right? Like there are like 40 methods we need to implement in PA1, but there are 10 public test cases, which some methods didn't even get tested. So now we have to guess how the outputs should look like, and pray ZINC has mercy on us.

2. It's a clusterfuck

PA1 and PA2 are fine, while PA1 is huge, you do know what you're doing. PA2 is fun, teaches you how to trade stock.

PA3 is uhhhhhhh, one of the PAs of all time. The task is to make a Efficient Database Simulator with a shit ton of trees and hashtables. Maybe I'm naive, but I don't think that's how database is implemented? Like the class data structure is there's an index tree where each node stores a binary tree and each node over there stores a hastable for storing entries, which idk, already exists in another hashtable in the class? If you don't undestand anything I just said, nobody does when doing the PA.

The feeling I'm getting from this PA is it is testing knowledge for the sake of testing it, so it's straight up unrealistic for an actual program to look like this. It took me 4 days with constant attention to finish the fucking thing.

comment_teaching:
Given that Desmond is not teaching COMP2012 in the near future due to some program requirement adjustments in CSE, this is just for reference:

If you wish to get a good grade, I would strongly recommend you to enrol in or sit in Desmond's class, what you would gain from his class is MORE than you can expect and worth it.

He has given us clearer explanations on the topics taught in the lecture notes, given us additional and supplementary exercises to practice at home, sent us emails to explain certain topics, and taught us something interesting that is not in the syllabus. Overall, his teaching is comprehensive.

You might want to argue that he shouldn't teach something out of the syllabus, but we are not here to study for good grades. If there is something interesting that may benefit us for our future, I'm willing to learn more, instead of just striving for an A range.

Though it's sad that my PAs 1 and 3 ended up like this (feel sorry about that tbh), Desmond is really a good instructor who cares about students, unlike some who just care about students' performance, but he also cares about our well-being. Instead of a professor, he's more like a mentor to me.

I won't be able to earn an A range in COMP 2012, but still, I've still managed to get a score higher than the UQ, which is totally unexpected, but the efforts and time paid are worth it, as a wise man once told me:
"It's okay to have a not-so-good grade, but at least you have learnt something. " (Tsoi, 2025)
Yes, my PA scores are sh*t, but I've proven myself that I've learnt a lot through the final exams, no matter what letter grade would I eventually get.

comment_content:
As you might have guessed from the title, I have a story to tell. Yeah, but I’m gonna save the details for the “workload” section if you are curious about it. For now, I just want to remind you to strictly adhere to the honor code and avoid plagiarism.

Right, let’s come back to the main topic.
content covered:
Lambda, Constructor/Destructor, Inheritance and Polymorphism, Generic Programming, Binary Search Tree, Hashing, rvalue Reference and Move Semantics.
Optional(briefly covered in lab tutorial, not tested):
make, namespace, static, Standard template Library(used in PA3)
2012 delves deeper into materials you have learnt in 2011, especially those introduced after 2011 midterm like pointers. Unlike 2011 which included IQ-style questions like recursion, 2012 focuses more on mastering ‘object-oriented programming’. You can expect greater emphasis on working with “heap”. So make sure to consider carefully about the memory allocation and wisely manage the structure when programming.
comment_teaching:

2, He tends to emphasize on some important or difficult parts and his pace is also a little bit fast. So it’s not very easy to stay focused throughout the entire class. But it’s definitely rewarding to attend his classes.

comment_workload:
The workload is even worse than 2011. So try not to take this course with other courses with a huge workload, otherwise life will be hard.
Lab: 8 labs in total. Most of them are fairly easy, but each still takes around 2 hours to finish. I’m not sure whether they can make some changes according to 2211, maybe 4 graded labs, 4 ungraded??
PA: 3 PAs in total. Each of them had some minor mistakes, taking 1-2 days to be fixed after it was released, so it feels like the teaching team wasn't very responsible. First two were manageable, each took around 3 days(15 hrs) to finish. The last one was designed by ‘notorious’ Brian(known for PAs made by him, hahaha). It took me 5 days(30 hrs) to finish that last PA. The structure of the PA was bizarre as hell: you have to build a Binary tree, which stores binary trees, which store hash tables, which store binary trees. 5 levels!!! If you want to know more about the structure, please refer to the attached file shared on Piazza(source: God Yu). The funny thing was that someone crashed out and swore at the teaching team directly on Piazza…Crazy.

My story:
I don’t want to blame my friends for what happened cuz I also hold responsibility, I just hope you can learn from my experience and avoid making the same mistake.
It was actually related to PA1: To be honest, there were a few functions in PA1 that were tricky to deal with. So after I finished, my friend reached out to me and asked questions about some functions. Since we chatted online, it was too hard to describe a 100-line function within minutes, so I explained with screenshots. A few days later, my friend came back again and asked for my code to cross check. He promised me he wouldn't copy it so I sent my file.
A month later, after finishing the 2012 midterm in the morning, I took a joyous nap. But I was shocked when I opened my mailbox afterward—you know what email I received. Panicked, I asked my friend for his last submission. At first glance, I thought it was a mistake cuz when I clicked ‘compared with selected’, the page was overwhelming with differences everywhere, even the indentation was different. I was furious that time like an innocent found guilty, thinking: diuu!!! Goofy plagiarism detection system, causing me troubles. However, that night, as I calmed down and analysed our programs, I noticed something suspicious: My friend had written some functions exactly according to my logic, line by line. The only differences were variable name and indentation… "OMG GGs." Deep copy is still copy, right?:) Upset? Annoyed? Frustrated? For a few nights after knowing that, I couldn’t fall asleep because of the worry.
Anyway, I appealed in hope of receiving a lighter penalty and went to a meeting with Desmond. To demonstrate how sophisticated the system is, Desmond used the system to check our work in front of me again. It flagged our work with a 90% similarity score. He told me that the system has already been in use since he was a student, and has been continually updated over time. So don't try your luck on a system that is probably older than you!(oh btw Desmond is a Young Guy!! :)) I pleaded for leniency from Desmond, he told me that it was also a difficult decision to impose a penalty on me, but for the sake of fairness and consistency in handling plagiarism cases, he couldn’t make exceptions. In the end, we agreed on the penalty: 0 for PA1 with additional 5 marks deducted from total.
Before the meeting ended, Desmond said something that has stuck with me ever since: "If your friend doesn't know what's wrong with their code and wants to see yours, tell them to ask us for help." So please, learn from my mistake: Do not share your code with anyone for any reason!!!
comment_grading:

Each of LQs is basically a condensed version of lab, with those long functions left to finish. So if you can finish labs on your own, you should be able to handle at least 60-70% of functions in LQs.

A heavy workload can arise from a combination of factors that impact individuals and teams in various settings. One primary reason is increased demand for products or services, which can lead to more tasks than usual due to seasonal trends, new projects, or expanding markets. Additionally, staff shortages often exacerbate the situation; when there are not enough employees to manage the workload, remaining staff may be required to take on additional responsibilities, resulting in heightened pressure and stress. Inefficient processes also play a significant role; outdated technologies or poorly designed workflows can create bottlenecks, leading to longer task durations and a backlog of work.

Moreover, the complexity of certain projects can contribute to a heavy workload. Tasks that involve technical challenges, regulatory requirements, or the need for collaboration among multiple teams often require more time and effort to complete. Poor time management can further complicate matters, as individuals may struggle with prioritizing tasks or managing their time effectively, leading to a buildup of work and missed deadlines. Frequent interruptions from meetings, emails, or other distractions can disrupt focus and extend the time needed to complete tasks. Unrealistic deadlines set without considering available resources can leave employees feeling rushed and overwhelmed, while a lack of support, such as insufficient training or resources, can leave them feeling unprepared to handle their responsibilities.

In summary, a heavy workload can significantly affect employee morale, productivity, and overall job satisfaction. Identifying the underlying causes is crucial for organizations to implement strategies that alleviate pressure and promote a more balanced and effective work environment.
comment_grading: Despite achieving a score above the mean, the grade received is a C+, which indicates a disconnect between performance and evaluation standards. This situation can be frustrating, as it suggests that while the individual's performance is better than average, it does not meet the higher benchmarks set for a more favorable grade. Factors contributing to this outcome may include the grading criteria being overly stringent, the distribution of scores among peers skewing the average, or the grading system itself not adequately reflecting the level of effort and understanding demonstrated. Such a result can lead to concerns about fairness and the effectiveness of the assessment methods used, prompting a reevaluation of how grades are assigned in relation to actual performance.

comment_teaching:
Why is Desmond the best instructor that I have ever learned from in HKUST?

In terms of technical efforts, It’s clear he has tried to make the tragic idea of sitting through or watching lectures as smooth as possible. Crisp and clear sounding mic? Done. Punctual lecture recording uploads? Done. Lecture recordings with mic connected to the audio? Done. Lecture recordings all compiled in a website? Done. Lecture recordings all compiled in a website plus progress of lecture slides (e.g. topic XYZ p.23-48) next to each video? Done. What more can I ask for? I have not once waited for him to upload his lecture videos if I missed class. And for revision, if I forgot some topic, I didn’t have to spend an hour opening fifteen tabs of zoom recordings to figure out which video it was.

Desmond is unparalleled, compared to most instructors. The others simply don’t pay attention to minute details like this that make it easier for students to consume their knowledge.

His teaching is also powerful. Each sentence he speaks is so dynamic and meaningful; he could literally become a voice actor. There are some professors who speak like a robot for half the lecture to explain a topic, and then suddenly express a slight hint of their quasi-personality to show that the topic they’ve taught is indeed, remarkable or interesting. Desmond doesn’t speak like a robot at all. He teaches with the curiosity of students in mind and in a way, he is sort of performing when he does so.

Aside from his oral delivery, his ideas are very intuitive. He is not reading lecture notes most of the time but instead giving these very simplified and dumbed-down programs. Surprisingly, his programs cover most of the lecture notes. He has consistently (and jokingly) thrown shade on some of Professor Mak’s complicated example programs that reside in the lecture notes, which I live for.

When Desmond teaches, he has shown immense empathy to the students. For one, I think he knows that people lose focus normally. Every time I lost focus in class, Desmond would literally repeat his latest sentence, word for word, as if he could sense that I was zoning out to another dimension. On a serious note, I think if I had some attention deficit, this class wouldn’t be that bad. This obviously may have increased the time of the lectures but I don’t think Desmond cares. I mean, it is clear that he isn’t there because he is a professor and needs to finish the syllabus before the end of the semester because it is his job. He is there because he wants to teach. Desmond also knows that students struggle and acknowledged the difficulty of the course on multiple occasions. He has made it so clear that help is found whenever needed and this made it much easier to complete this course.  

Desmond also has a website where you can see his weekly schedule. If you can’t make it to his office hours, and you missed TA’s office hours, you can email him to perhaps book an appointment and your name will be on the schedule. Like what? This guy is just so detailed.

Overall, I think Desmond is an excellent teacher and he knows that. I hope he never stops teaching.

comment_content:
Asides from teaching you many useful C++ features, I think a big focus is on language concepts and how to explain various C++ standards e.g. why shouldn’t you declare a pure virtual destructor without implementation in a class hierarchy?, why can’t you use a rvalue reference to initialize another rvalue reference ? etc.

Of cause as the course title suggests, they will also teach you some useful data Structures like BST & hash table and you will have plenty opportunities to practice applying those data structures in PA and labs.

You’ll find out in other courses many more application of those data structures like hash table can be used to improve run time of sorting. Overall the content is very important and interesting.

Exams are quite competitive, in addition to assessing your mastery on course material and problem solving skills, they also test your stamina ie. Can you code for long hour even when you’re tired? exam is ~3 hrs you know.

Also in exam time allocation is key, I got low mark in final because I spent too much time challenging the hard question and rushing through easy questions, this resulted in tons of careless mistakes. So allocate your time more rationally.

Speaking of the final exam, in the last question (AVL), If you have very good concept of pointers (COMP2011 topic) I think you can solve the problem without using any AVL technique which is out syllabus. So make sure you do through review on pointer and how they may be creatively used.
comment_teaching:
Very good teaching from Desmond, he explained complicated topics in a way you can understand e.g. when he taught move semantics, instead of following the slide he gave us the big picture (resource stealing) first, he also illustrated using actual examples.

Also his passion in teaching is highly contagious, you'll suddenly be very motivated too when you attend his lecture lol

TA team is also very good, especially Dinh Anh Dung (the lead TA i guess) he answer questions on Piazza very fast and concise. I also like the 2 PAs he designed which is PA2 tournament matching and PA3 tic tac toe with minimax, at least for me they're really fun.

In PA3, interestingly the TA made an important logical mistake in the hash table optimization for alpha-beta pruning that made the algorithm incorrect,  however the mistake is ... super tricky and technical, so (unsurprisingly) no one (a whole class of few hundred people!) found out until the TA later found out himself lol. But that error is really just an exception, they don't make mistake 99% of the time. Still when you're doing assignment, you should think more critically and actually understand the purpose of the code and the logic behind it. I too thought I'd throughly understood the code, but I still couldn't spot that huge mistake, so I actually didn't throughly understand the code ... lol

There're around 700 questions on Piazza and only 13 unanswered. So the teaching team did a great job.
comment_workload:
5 mins lab attendance each week and  3 PAs. On average, you can finish labs within 1 hour PA within a day, but I always spent additional time to triple check and make sure there's no mistake.

Also do more past papers to train intuition on exam questions, this's key to answer questions very fast in exam. Note that intuition != logical thinking and there're good videos on Youtube explaining how to take advantage intuition in competitive exams. But over reliance on intuition may backfire as it's error prone.

3 PAs. Each PA takes around 1-2 days to work on. As almost half of the class get full in every PA, you eventually will have to work your ass off to save your grade.

To conclude, work hard if you register this course. It will be fruitful

comment_teaching: Desmond focuses on the logic of each piece of knowledge(in his word, xxx is sth beautiful) and likes to repeat some important points(sometimes in English, Putonghua and Cantonese) and demo important procedures like ("Can we go into the body now? No, we have to first initialize all its data member!",  bst node delete bst tree, node, tree, node,tree....) This teaching style really facilitates my understanding of new stuff. Honestly speaking, although the content in comp2012 is a bit less harder than the pointer part in 2011 when you first learn it, the overall difficulties is much harder than 2011. Although some students with good foundations and talent may find that the speed a little bit low, I first think that if desmond spent less time in saying sth like " do you want to learn sth new please say yes!", we can speed up our teaching progress and there will be no addition class( I don't really hate addition class but when it is conducted on Fri evening or the day when you have a whole day class it really breaks your mood.), when the difficulties of the content is increasing, it acts as a buffer for you to recall and catch up. The power of brainwashing is so strong and I believe everyone who takes his class will remember some typical lines.Another feature of his class is that he encourages us to response and ask questions in class and it is really beneficial to learn from classmates' questions. Maybe for some students they don't like this kind of hyperbole, but for students who get below mean in 1021 this teaching style really enables us to have a better understanding of new content and builds a good foundation.
comment_workload:
The workload is nearly the same as comp2011(may be slightly heavier because of pa2)
The labs are easier than 2011.
For pa1, it requires us to think of some algorithms which means the difficulties don't lie in how to write a constructor...
For pa2, whether you know where to add a virtual keyword or add public inheritance does no help to the solution. We have to read a lot of .h files to guess what the game rules and how to implement it. Although I know that the intention is good because it is what real life does and I do revere the hard work of the designer who spends a month to design this game, there are still some bugs and umm the clue is a little less and in Chinese, it is sth like 对牛弹琴 because the designer thinks we can understand him but it is obvious that we haven't reached that stage, and finally, this homework turns into a task filling assignment with clear instruction and it loses all its meanings and this assignment becomes totally a failure.
For pa3, it is just some basic training of BST and template so it is much easier than the first two assignments and it is really nice when there are lots of ddl approaching final.
For exams I do recommend doing past papers because desmond likes to follow the style of the past.

comment_teaching:
Simply excellent, Desmond is the best instructor you can possibly hope to get in a programming course, he gives many examples and constantly asks questions to make sure you understand what he is saying, and even repeats in 3 different languages just so he can explain his ideas more clearly.

He also sends a lot of emails out to further explain the concepts. One of the most enthusiastic lecturers I have ever seen in UST.

Lab sessions are conducted orderly and swiftly, the TAs do a good job.
comment_workload:
OK for a 4 credit course.

Labs do not take very long to complete and is not too difficult, often its just completing a simple class definition.

Assignments are also manageable, however time needs to be spent debugging and making sure you are ready for all of the test cases. In my opinion, 2012 has easier assignments than 2011, since what you need to do is more clear in this course.

Exams require you to be very clear on the concepts related to the course, and doing the past papers is extremely important since the questions in the exam are closely related down to the way they are asked. However some questions rely on your programming skills, and no amount of studying and doing past papers can help there.
comment_grading:
3 Programming assignments (10% each): 3 assignments, each about a different area, (for this semester: class, inheritance and BST), difficulty is acceptable other than assignment 2 where the TA made the assignment far too complex and had to spend about a week reworking the assignment to make it easier.

Laboratory exercises (10%): 1 lab per week, finish the code and attend the session, you may be asked a question by the TAs regarding the lab, the questions are mostly easy and TAs are lenient with the score, not difficult to get full marks here.

Midterm (20%): True or False questions and short answers, mostly about constructors and destructors. Doing the past papers here is key.

Final examination (40%): Same as the midterm, covers the entire course equally, again doing the past papers is key.

I got slightly above mean in the midterm, mean in finals, attended all labs and got around 95% in all 3 assignments, ended up with a B, decent grading.

comment_content: C++ foundation+OOP+DS, the outline is the same as 2011+2012, but it goes deeper and more detailed. As a C++ course without algorithms, the algorithms previously learned by the competition students will basically not be used, and the data structure will only learn a more basic part, and new learning OOP (about 50%) is required.
